verses.commentary: Jesus ascends a mountain to teach — a new Moses on a new Sinai. He sits, the posture of the authoritative rabbi. Matthew structures his Gospel around five teaching blocks echoing the five books of Moses; this is the first.
'Opened his mouth' (anoixas to stoma, ἀνοίξας τὸ στόμα) — solemn formula for authoritative discourse (Job 3:1; Psalm 78:2). The Sermon begins.

Makarios (μακάριος, blessed) — not happy but congratulated by God, possessed of a divine favor visible only at the end. Poor in spirit: those whose interior is destitute, who know they bring nothing to God. The kingdom is theirs — present tense.

Those who mourn — over sin, over loss, over the world's brokenness. Comfort (paraklethesontai, παρακληθήσονται) echoes Isaiah 61:2 — the Messiah comes 'to comfort all who mourn.' Eschatological promise.

Meek (praeis, πραεῖς) — Psalm 37:11 verbatim. Not weakness but power under control, refusing to grasp. Inherit the earth (gen, γῆν) — the meek receive the very thing the world tries to seize. Kingdom economics inverts.

Hunger and thirst for righteousness (dikaiosyne, δικαιοσύνη) — ravenous appetite for God's justice. Filled (chortazo, χορτάζω) — gorged, satisfied. Spiritual desire is the one appetite God promises to over-satisfy.
Merciful receive mercy. Reciprocity — not earning, but congruence with God's character (compare 6:14-15; 18:33). Mercy given is mercy known.

Pure in heart (katharoi te kardia, καθαροὶ τῇ καρδίᾳ) — Psalm 24:4. Interior unified, undivided loyalty. They will see God — the supreme blessing, language reserved for the eschatological vision (1 John 3:2).
Peacemakers (eirenopoioi, εἰρηνοποιοί) — not passive but active reconcilers. Called sons of God — they bear the family resemblance to the Father who reconciles. Identity by activity.

Persecuted for righteousness (heneken dikaiosynes, ἕνεκεν δικαιοσύνης) — the eighth beatitude bookends with the first (kingdom of heaven). Eight blessings; the eight echoes resurrection / new creation.
Shift to second person: 'Blessed are you.' Persecution becomes personal — 'because of me.' Jesus places himself at the center of the disciples' identity and suffering.
Rejoice and leap for joy (agalliasthe, ἀγαλλιᾶσθε). The persecuted disciples stand in prophetic succession — Jeremiah, Elijah, Zechariah. Suffering authenticates the calling.
Salt — preservation, purification, covenant marker (Leviticus 2:13). Saltless salt is useless. The disciples must remain pungent or be discarded. Identity is functional, not nominal.

Light of the world — title Jesus elsewhere applies to himself (John 8:12). Disciples derive light from him and radiate it derivatively. City on a hill: Zion's eschatological role to draw nations (Isaiah 2:2-3).
Lamp under bowl is absurd — the entire point is to be seen. Disciples are designed for visibility, not concealment. Hidden light betrays its purpose.
Good works visible — but glorifying the Father, not the doer. Visibility serves the wrong end when it glorifies self (compare 6:1). The aim sanctifies the display.
Pleroo (πληρῶσαι, fulfill) not abolish. Jesus completes the Law and Prophets — brings them to their intended goal. He is the trajectory's endpoint.
Iota (ἰῶτα, smallest Hebrew letter yod) and serif (keraia, κεραία, smallest stroke). Until heaven and earth pass. The Law's permanence is total — but its fulfillment, not its repetition, is the goal.
Status in the kingdom tracks fidelity to commandments — even the least. Teaching shapes ranking. Antinomianism is downgraded; obedient teaching is exalted.

Righteousness must exceed (perisseuse, περισσεύσῃ) the Pharisees and scribes — Israel's most rigorous moralists. Not better compliance but a different kind of righteousness, anchored in the heart. The bar moves higher, not lower.
First of six antitheses ('You have heard… but I say'). Jesus does not contradict Moses but penetrates surface compliance. Murder begins long before the act.

Anger, contempt ('Raca,' ῥακά, empty-head), and verbal aggression ('Fool') escalate to Sanhedrin and Gehenna (γέεννα — the smoldering Hinnom valley). Sin is rooted in the heart's posture toward the brother.
Worship and reconciliation are entangled. The altar pauses for relationship. Vertical worship cannot run on broken horizontals.
Leave gift, go reconcile, return. Sequence matters. Worship is interrupted, not abandoned — but the brother's grievance takes priority over the offering.
Quick settlement before court. The legal frame parallels the ultimate court before God. Reconcile now, while opportunity lasts.
Down to the last quadrans (kodrantes, κοδράντης, smallest Roman coin). Total payment required. The warning is eschatological: settle accounts before final judgment.
Second antithesis: adultery. The seventh commandment quoted (Exodus 20:14).
Lustful look already commits adultery in the heart (kardia, καρδία). The internal disposition is the moral location — bodies merely execute what the heart already willed.
Hyperbole: pluck out the eye. Better mutilation than damnation. Sin is a survival emergency. Jesus is not commanding self-harm but seriousness.
Same with the hand. Repetition for emphasis. Whatever generates sin — sever the connection. The body is not an end but a servant.
Third antithesis: divorce. Citation of Deuteronomy 24:1 — the certificate of divorce concession.
Divorce 'except for sexual immorality' (porneia, πορνεία). Permissive divorce becomes serial adultery. Jesus tightens what Moses allowed for hardness of heart (compare 19:8).
Fourth antithesis: oaths. The pious habit of swearing to authenticate speech.
Do not swear at all. Heaven is God's throne — invoking it is invoking him.
Earth is footstool (Isaiah 66:1). Jerusalem the city of the Great King (Psalm 48:2). Every oath drags God in by the back door.
Cannot make one hair white or black — humans control nothing they swear by. Oaths are pretensions of authority we don't possess.
'Yes' and 'No' suffice. Anything more comes from the evil one (ek tou ponerou, ἐκ τοῦ πονηροῦ). Truthful speech makes oaths redundant; oath-need exposes background dishonesty.
Fifth antithesis: lex talionis. Exodus 21:24 — eye for eye. Originally a limit on vengeance (proportional, not escalating). Jesus moves further: not vengeance at all.

Do not resist (antistenai, ἀντιστῆναι) the evil one — when struck on the right cheek (a backhand insult, more humiliating than injurious), offer the other. Defiant non-retaliation that exposes the violence.
Sue for tunic — give cloak too. To stand without outer garment in court is to shame the unjust system. Generosity weaponized against avarice.
Forced to go one mile (Roman soldier's conscription right) — go two. Voluntary excess subverts coercion. The kingdom converts compulsion into gift.
Give to the asker, do not refuse the borrower. Open-handedness as standard posture. Not transactional, not calculated by deserving.
Sixth antithesis: love neighbor (Leviticus 19:18) — but the addition 'hate your enemy' is from popular interpretation, not Torah. Jesus corrects the gloss.
Love your enemies (agapate tous echthrous, ἀγαπᾶτε τοὺς ἐχθρούς). Pray for persecutors. The most countercultural command — love directed at those who harm. Distinctive of the Christian ethic.
Sons of the Father — bear his character. He sends sun and rain on evil and good alike. Common grace defines impartiality. Children imitate the parent.
Reciprocal love earns no reward — even tax collectors (collaborators with Rome) love their own. Christian distinctiveness must clear that floor.
Greeting only one's own people is mere ethnic warmth. Even Gentiles do that. Christian community must transcend natural affinity.

Teleioi (τέλειοι, perfect/complete/mature). Not flawlessness but completeness in love that imitates the Father (Luke's parallel: 'be merciful'). The sermon's interior climax: the disciples' character finally resembles God's.
